---
title: "Data-Analysis-Project-R"
author: "Myojeong Kim"
date: "2026-04-22"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Hypothesis 1.
Is there an association between celebrity association with a beauty product and consumers’ purchase intention?



```{r}
library(dplyr)
data <- read_excel('./Responses.xlsx')
```

```{r}
data <- data %>%
  rename(
    Q8  = `8. I am more interested in a beauty product if it is associated with a celebrity.`,
    Q10 = `10. Celebrity involvement makes a product more appealing.`,
    Q13 = `13. I am likely to purchase a beauty product endorsed by a celebrity.`)

```

```{r}
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    celebrity_association = rowMeans(select(.,Q8,Q10), na.rm = TRUE), purchase_intention = rowMeans(select(.,Q13), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data, aes(x = celebrity_association, y = purchase_intention)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    title = "Celebrity Association and Purchase Intention",
    x = "Celebrity Association",
    y = "Purchase Intention"
  )+
  theme_minimal()

```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
